headline: Engaged learning with mind-wandering — the classroom-attention signature.
synthesis_paragraph: About a minute of a classroom lecture from a student's perspective, and the neural trace tells exactly the story you'd expect from real-world learning — engaged with intermittent mind-wandering, not the unbroken focus of a movie or the locked perception of a field-stress clip. The opening seconds are somatomotor-dominant (t=0-7s), reading as postural settling into the seat. Visual takes over from t=10-20s as the student tracks the instructor and the projector. The middle stretch is where it gets specifically interesting: default-mode incursions appear at t=25s and t=50s — brief windows of self-referential drift, the cognitive equivalent of looking out the window — but each time the trace returns to focus, with frontoparietal peaking at 0.31 around t=45s in what looks like a moment of active working-memory load, perhaps following a specific lecture point. The closing seconds (t=55-58s) show ventral-attention rising — a salience event near the end, possibly the instructor wrapping up or a slide change drawing fresh attention. Limbic stayed near baseline throughout — the content was academic, not emotionally charged, and the trace reflects that cleanly.

temporal_arc.description: Somatomotor-dominant — postural settling into the seat.
Visual takes over as the student tracks the instructor and the projector.
Dorsal-attention then frontoparietal peak (0.31) — active working-memory load on a specific lecture point.
Second default-mode incursion — another mind-wandering moment.
Ventral-attention rises — a salience event near the close, possibly slide change or instructor wrap-up.

neural_evidence: Frontoparietal peak (0.31) at t=45s — the highest non-perceptual peak in the clip.
Default-mode incursions at t=25s and t=50s — brief mind-wandering windows.
Limbic peak (0.06) — academic content, no affective charge.
Ventral-attention rises in the final 3 seconds — closing salience event.
inflection_moment: t=45s — frontoparietal peaks at 0.31, the highest non-perceptual engagement of the clip, suggesting an active working-memory moment as the student processes a specific lecture point.
scenario_lens: consumer · educational · classroom learning with mind-wandering windows
